I think youıll find more than enough moneyed wizarding families eager to throw gold at a fund like this for the good publicity.Ë McGonagall nodded again. ÊI quite agree, an excellent suggestion. I will add that detail for the governors.Ë She made a note on a separate piece of parchment. Draco could feel the Calming Draught begin to wear off, and was startled at the time when he looked at his watch, noting it was nearing lunch. ÊThere is just one more matter, Mr. Malfoy.Ë Fucking Salazar Slytherin, what now? ÊWhen do you intend to inform Miss Granger of her eponymous fund? I cannot imagine she would be overly thrilled to read of it in the Prophet. Nor do I think she would appreciate being left in the dark while this is approved without her input.Ë Fuck, McGonagall was right. Granger would be rightly hacked off at him if she knew heıd gone behind her back and handed her brainchild off to the Ministry after a bunch of old school governors had their opportunity for a say. But he couldnıt have her trying to stop him either. ÊRight before Ministry sign-off.Ë Draco said firmly. ÊShe can approve the final version or scrap it all then if she wants. But I donıt want to get her hopes up if it doesnıt even make it to that stage.Ë McGonagall pursed her lips, and Draco could tell she didnıt approve of this under-handed way of dealing with Granger. Eventually she nodded her assent, and stood with her hand outstretched. ÊWell Mr. Malfoy, this was a rather pleasant surprise of a visit. You should have an owl from me before the month is over.Ë Draco stood quickly and shook her hand. Then his eyes flicked upward and he tried not to gasp. Dumbledoreıs portrait was now quite awake and smiling benignly down at him. But what caused Dracoıs breath to catch was the steady, fixed glare of two familiar, intense beetle-black eyes, boring into his own. Draco almost put up his occlumency shields in his mind before remembering that a portrait of Severus Snape could not penetrate his mind, no matter how accurate the likeness. Iım sorry. Iım sorry. Iım sorry, Iım so fucking sorry. McGonagall gave him an understanding sort of grimace as Draco tore his gaze away from the two previous headmasters. ÊLunch is about ready to be served in the Great Hall. Would you care to join me at the staff table?Ë Draco shook his head, knowing his Calming Draught had run its course. There was no way heıd be able to sit through a meal with the rest of his old professors and the eyes of hundreds of students on him. ÊThank you Headmistress, but I think I should be heading home. I can see myself out.Ë Draco made it to just outside the office door before his shaking hands quickly closed around the vial of Calming Draught. He stopped and tipped the rest of the potion back. On the other side of the door, he heard the amused voice of Dumbledore remark, ÊI did tell you once before, didnıt I Severus? I truly believe we sort students far too young.Ë Draco couldnıt make sense of the comment but could have sworn he heard a snarl in response and the familiar sound of Snapeıs swishing black robes as Draco pictured him scowling and sweeping out of frame. ÊWell, did I deliver on my word or not? Didnıt I tell you this would be spectacular? She does this after every single match.Ë Draco nodded, impressed, as he watched the young player gently complete her descent down to land on the quidditch pitch. With the exception of the young woman shouldering her broom down below, the rest of the pitch was deserted. Draco had finally made good on his word to hang around with Wesley Macnair after a match. Considering that Dracoıs Wimbourne Wasps had just wiped the floor with Macnairıs Tornadoes, Draco figured the least he could do was linger behind to see what Macnair was so keen to show off. As the rest of the company box and the crowd gathered in the stadium emptied, Macnair had lifted his omnioculars to his face again and directed Draco to do the same. ÊTrust me, youıll want to see this.Ë A young woman in a Tornadoes jersey had remained on the pitch with her broom and a Beaterıs bat in hand. She kicked off suddenly, and then pointed her wand behind her to release two bludgers from their crate. The two balls shot at her at a ferocious speed, but she batted them away quickly as she flew higher into the air. What followed for the next half hour was a relentless assault by the bludgers and the flurry of the playerıs bat as she beat back attempt after attempt of the balls to unseat her. Draco watched as her brow was furrowed in concentration, sweat beginning to flow down the sides of her face as she ferociously swung her bat with an incredible strength and precision. He had never seen this impressive a combination of grace, control, and brute strength. Her shoulders were broad and Draco could see the outline of her bulging biceps even through her jersey. It was clear that she was in spectacular shape and at the distance the bludgers traveled after she whacked them, she wielded her strength expertly. He heard a soft chuckle next to him as Macnair broke through his thoughts as he watched the young player take her leave of the pitch. ÊAll right, explain. Why is that girl not in your starting lineup?Ë Macnair frowned and sighed. ÊYou know the Tornadoes, itıs been a boyıs club for their entire existence. That young lass there, Maureen Tyler, has been a reserve Beater for the last two seasons, and you donıt even know how many times Iıve begged the coaches and captain to promote her to a starter.Ë Draco nodded in understanding. The Tornadoes liked to think of themselves as the all-male answer to the Holyhead Harpies which was obviously moronic, in Dracoıs opinion. It was nothing of an official ban against female players, of course, but when the only female player in several decades couldnıt seem to break out of the reserve pool, well it seemed the whispers about the coaches and team atmosphere were proven correct. ÊSo why have you revealed this great secret weapon to me? The Wasps have no room for more Beaters on the squad, Iıd be laughed out of the room for suggesting it.Ë Macnair nodded and pressed on ÊTrue, lad, I donıt think sheıs a fit with you either. But can you think of another team that might benefit from her superior skills?Ë Draco shook his head, not in ignorance but in disagreement. Now he understood where Macnair was heading. ÊNo way, Iıd never be able to convince McLaggen. Heıs only the scout for the Harpies so he can play Èhide-the-wandı with the more attractive scouting prospects. Besides, you know this type of collusion is frowned upon by Bellamy, right?Ë Macnair waved an impatient hand. ÊCome off it, you canıt tell me that girl isnıt a bloody beating prodigy! She would be starting on any other squad!Ë Draco sized up his colleague and narrowed his eyes. ÊWhatıs it to you? Why are you so concerned over this girl?Ë Macnair sighed and ran a hand through his dark stubble. ÊLook, I recruited her right out of Hogwarts. Iım serious when I tell you Iıve never seen a talent for beating like hers. And well╔ sheıs Muggleborn and I donıt think she had a lot of career options after school, to be honest with you. She told me she just wants to bring in enough money to support her father.Ë Dracoıs frown deepened and he ran a hand impatiently through his own white-blond hair. Why was it his problem that Macnair had developed a soft spot for some Muggleborn girl? Hear that Malfoy? Thatıs the sound of the cauldron calling the kettle black╔ Fucking hell. ÊLook, if she means that much to you, then you talk to McLaggen.Ë ÊI canıt, I almost came to blows with him last month because he was sexually harassing Bellamyıs assistant. You went to school with him, right? See if you canıt turn on that old Malfoy charm, you know heıs a sucker for society climbing.Ë Draco let out a frustrated sigh, knowing Macnair had a point. That stupid ponce was so fucking transparent with his social aspirations and would do anything to be associated with either the wealthy, famous, or powerful. ÊFine I can try. Can you arrange a meeting with Tyler?Ë Macnair smirked. ÊNo time like the present, son. Sheıll be back out of the locker room in about three minutes.Ë And so Draco found himself leaning against the wall of the darkened stadium tunnel, waiting for Maureen Tyler to emerge. When she finally exited, dressed in a Muggle tracksuit, Draco gave what he hoped was a friendly nod. ÊMiss Tyler?Ë Her face turned into a frown and she crossed her arms in front of her chest. "Can I help you mate?" Draco approached her confidently and held out his hand. "Draco Malfoy, I don't know if you're familiarÐ?" "Yeah I know all about you." Draco cocked his head in confusion and retracted his outstretched hand. What the fuck was that supposed to mean? "You're the scout for the Wasps. The whole league knows who you are." She continued bluntly and Draco tried not to exhale in relief. Given that she was just 19 or 20, their paths at Hogwarts would never have crossed. Which meant he could play the part of the discerning scout. "Right then, so why don't I know you? Is it normal for a reserve player to linger after a season-ending match just to get some more practice swings in?" Maureen shrugged and tugged on one of the long, black braids from her ponytail. "Not sure why that's any of your business." Fine, if you want to be a brat about this. "I don't think your talents are being put to good use on the Tornadoes. I'm here to see if you're open to an alternative." Maureen Tyler shrugged again and switched to fidgeting with the broom handle slung over her shoulder. Nervous and trying not to show it. "Your Wasps don't need me. What can you actually offer me? I make good money with the Tornadoes and I'm not interested in messing about." Draco nodded in agreement. "I respect that. I'm merely asking if an opportunity from another team happened to come your way, would you be open to it? I'm talking about a starting position as a Beater, not wasting away on the bench and settling for secret practice sessions after the real match." She shrugged non-committedly again but Draco saw the slight gleam of hope in her eyes. "I'd think on it." That was good enough for Draco. He conjured his business card from thin air and handed it to her, only slightly surprised when she actually accepted it. ÊLet me know if I can be of any future service. Good evening, Miss Tyler.Ë Irksome good deed done for the evening, Draco apparated home and wrote Macnair a quick letter. He really needed to stop getting involved in things that were none of his business. At least he had tomorrow morning with Granger to look forward to. She was supposed to spend time with her parents this weekend. What did Granger get up to with her Muggle parents? Draco always politely inquired about her weekend activities, but noticed her answers were much shorter when it came to her own blood relations. She could wax rhapsodic about her meals and celebrations with the bloody Weasley clan, but when it came to the Grangers, Draco noticed a tightness around her eyes and in the way she spoke. Maybe he wasnıt the only one with a difficult parental situation. Maybe it was just one more, sad thing they had in common. Chapter End Notes A/N: Iıve loved hearing from you lovely readers along the way, so thank you so much for your comments/follows/faves, feedback is always appreciated. Iım always down to discuss Dramione (or HP in general) so drop a comment or a PM if youıre so inclined! Stay safe in the world- Jude Chapter 8: Chapter 8 Chapter Notes See the end of the chapter for notes November 2007 He could do this. He could totally, completely do this. It was absolutely within the capabilities of Draco Malfoy to ask Hermione Granger if she would like to grab dinner some night this weekend. He would be the height of casual when he asked. As friends. Well, almost friends. What would you call the pair of them? It was Dracoıs monthly appointment and Healer Browning had finally convinced Draco that this was becoming ridiculous. It of course didnıt prevent him from lashing out at the older man. ÊIf you think Iım acting childish, then bloody well say so!Ë Draco seethed at him. Browning merely raised an eyebrow over his spectacles and met Dracoıs furious glare with one of patience. ÊDo you think youıre acting childish?Ë Draco threw up his hands in frustration. ÊWell I donıt fucking know, thatıs why I come to you! All I want to know is how Iım supposed to ask her to dinner!Ë Browning met him with another calm stare, not once giving in to Dracoıs emotional outbursts. ÊYou use your words Draco, a simple, straightforward question.Ë ÊBut I donıt want her to think this is uh, a╔ date or anything╔Ë ÊYou donıt?Ë ÊNo.Ë Liar. Wait, what? ÊNo,Ë Draco repeated firmly, ignoring the little voice in his head. ÊA platonic outing. I want to see if sheıs open to that╔ because you know, we spend all those mornings together and it feels odd that we havenıt╔ progressed beyond that.Ë Because apparently progressing in friendship with Granger is something I want now. I am in control of this. Scratch, scratch, scratch went the floating quill. ÊWell Draco, I have to admit I think this is a phenomenal step forward for you. Inviting someone out for an activity without a guarantee that they will accept shows a willingness to be vulnerable.Ë Draco shrugged and tried not to shudder at the healerıs mention of vulnerability. Because thatıs exactly what Draco was in the presence of Hermione Granger. Overwhelmingly vulnerable. Monday morning came and went. Every time Draco opened his mouth to ask Hermione about dinner on the weekend, some stupid, banal question popped out instead. On Tuesday, Granger commented on how tired he looked and bought him an extra cup of coffee unprompted, but did he thank her and ask her to dinner then? No, he snapped at her like a surly prick to mind her own sleeping habits, and mulishly drank the coffee she brought him. To her credit, she only briefly smirked when he quietly thanked her as they walked to work. Wednesday was definitely the morning that Draco was going to pose the question of a weekend dinner. But then Hermione went into one of her passionate monologues about using her Ancient Runes knowledge in studying and relating to some of the oldest Mer-people tribes in the Mediterranean and Draco found himself rapt with attention as she chattered all the way down the street. By the time she waved goodbye, he remembered that he only had two days left in the week to accomplish his seemingly simple task. And now it was already Thursday and Draco still hadnıt asked Hermione about her weekend plans. ÊHave you ever visited Venice?Ë Her question interrupted the inner pep talk he was giving himself and sufficiently distracted him for the moment. ÊThis upcoming spring thereıs an academic conference on the communication methods of magical civilizations and you have to submit a formal proposal for your attendance. They usually donıt send someone from my department, you know, because Merfolk liaisons have their own office in the Department for International Magical Cooperation, but given my interest in Ancient RunesÐË ÊDonıt you mean your utter brilliance and unmatched skills in Ancient Runes?Ë He interrupted with a smirk. Hermione flushed prettily at his compliment. ÊWell, I donıt know about all that...Ë she muttered bashfully and bit lightly on her smiling lips. ÊWhat about Venice?Ë Draco steered the conversation back to the original topic so she might stop biting her lower lip and he could go back to ignoring that his stomach gave a curious flutter at her reaction to his words. ÊRight, so as I was saying, Iım submitting a request to attend the Venice conference to my department head and if that gets approved then I have to work on my submission to the conference review committee, and do you recall yesterday what I was telling you about the ancient Mer-people tribes?Ë ÊI do,Ë Draco cut in eagerly. ÊYou were saying that some of the oldest Mer-colonies were only recently discovered off the Italian coast and that some of them didnıt even speak Mermish but rather resorted to runes carved on their dwellings and stone tablets to communicate.Ë ÊThatıs right!Ë And she beamed at him. For Salazarıs sake, did no one in Grangerıs life listen to her when she spoke? She always looked like she wanted to bestow him with an Order of Merlin, First Class, any time he was able to recall something sheıd previously said in conversation. ÊThe conference is a whole week, and I hope to have at least some free time in the mornings or evenings to explore the city. I know they have one of the oldest magical libraries there with scrolls fromÐË ÊGranger, seriously? Youıre going to Venice to hole up in the library?Ë ÊWell then tell me what I should go see then,Ë she answered with a withering glare. ÊI take it youıve been before?Ë ÊOf course,Ë he sneered, though these days it lacked any malice behind it, at least when it came to Granger. ÊIıll give you a list of restaurants and wineries. And if you donıt make time to visit the Bridge of Two Suns then donıt bother going to Venice at all.Ë ÊOoh, whatıs that? Iıve never heard of it!Ë ÊWhatıs that Granger? Did you just admit I know something you donıt?Ë he drawled and earned himself an eye roll. ÊIım not sure of what the Italians call it, but thereıs a bridge over one of the canals that operates under some very ancient magic. If you stand on the bridge during sunset and face east, a sort of viewing portal appears and you can see tomorrow morningıs sunrise. No one really understands the magic behind it, but the locals use it as a sort of weather predictor for the following day.Ë She was staring at him while he spoke with that sort of wide-eye fascination and slightly parted lips that set off a curious reaction in his gut as of late. ÊWow, it sounds beautiful, thanks for the recommendation!Ë And then there was that. The constant thanking him for the simplest of things. Fuck, youıd think heıd given her one of his kidneys by the sincere way she thanked him all the time for just talking with her or getting her tea or recommending a wine. ÊWe should get going, I think,Ë Grangerıs voice broke through his inner monologue and Draco began to panic. His task of asking her to dinner remained unfinished and he was almost out of time. They walked in companionable silence to their normal separation point in front of the Leaky Cauldron, but internally, Draco was a nervous wreck. ÊSee you tomorrow Malfoy!Ë she chirped and began walking away. Do it now, do it now, do it now. I am in control of this. ÊHey, Granger, wait!Ë She turned around and gave him a curious look. The brisk November breeze played with the ends of her curls and Draco counted the little puffs of her breath made visible in the cold morning air. He then counted several heart beats before he closed the short distance between them, feeling more foolish with each step. ÊTomorrowıs Friday.Ë Wow. Smooth. She gave him a confused stare at his obvious declaration. ÊYes. It is.Ë If someone could just Avada him right now, that would be awfully convenient. He took a deep lungful of chilly air. ÊRight, well, do you have any plans? In the evening, I mean, obviously youıre working during the day.Ë Salazarıs fucking arse, could he be any more awkward? Granger continued her stare of confusion. Come on Granger, put it together, please put me out of my fucking misery here. ÊEr, no I donıt. Why do you ask?Ë Damn it all, he really was going to have to say it. Coward. Another deep breath. ÊIf youıre free in the evening, which I guess you are, because you just said╔ would you be up for grabbing some dinner out?Ë Her eyes became impossibly wide in shock and Draco wished he could stuff the words right back in his mouth and then obliviate the both of them. That not being a realistic option, he tried to desperately remedy the situation. ÊIt would just be to talk over dinner, not like a╔ uhh╔ well, we could talk more about Venice without having to rush off to make it to work on time.Ë Heıd been uncomfortably close to using the word Êdate.Ë Why was he such a bumbling fool all of a sudden? She still couldnıt seem to shake off her shocked expression. ÊYou want to have dinner together?Ë Draco rolled his eyes mockingly and made a stab at his usual sardonic tone. ÊWhy yes, Granger, I do believe thatıs what I asked. Will you join me or not?Ë She finally quit gaping at him like a fish and regained some of her composure. ÊAll right then. What time?Ë Merlinıs beard╔ that meant yes, right? Ê7?Ë Draco had already made a reservation, but he wasnıt about to let that slip. ÊWhere?Ë ÊHave you been to The Wilting Rose? Itıs in the theatre district.Ë It was one of the only restaurants Draco frequented anymore in Diagon Alley. ÊI havenıt! I suppose Iıll be trying it with you then! Still on for coffee tomorrow morning?Ë ÊOf course Granger.Ë ÊSee you Malfoy!Ë Was he imagining it or did her smile look a little brighter as she turned to leave? Draco shook his head and made his own way to work and tried to ignore the fact that he felt lighter than he had in years. Itıs just dinner. One dinner. I am in control of this. Of course Hermione Granger arrived early. Draco found her fidgeting nervously in her professional, gray robes by the host stand of the restaurant. Her hair was spilling down over her shoulders now, as opposed to this morning in the coffee shop when sheıd had it neatly pinned back. Draco wondered if this is how she looked by the end of every work day: her wild waves no longer contained as they came undone, lock by soft lock, out of the style Granger tried to tame them with each morning for the office. For his part, Draco had immediately gone home after work and changed into more formal robes and a fresh black suit. He didnıt dare spend time analyzing the fact that it took him far longer than usual to decide on what to wear for dinner tonight. He was in front of the large vanity of his bedroom fussing with his hair when he caught a glimpse of his watch and realized he would be late if he didnıt stop messing about with his looks like a vapid socialite. This was just dinner with Granger after all, no need to obsess over his appearance so much. When Hermione turned toward him and shot him a relieved smile, Draco took note of the hostıs suspicious face casting a surly look at Hermione behind her back. Draco could have kicked himself. The reason he was still welcome at this particular establishment was because in the years before the war, he used to frequent The Wilting Rose with his parents. The Malfoy family had been a most welcome presence in this pureblood sympathizing business, frequented by many members of the Sacred Twenty-Eight in its heyday. Furious with himself for so easily falling back on the comforts from his old life, Draco drew himself up to his full height as he approached the host. ÊGranger,Ë he greeted her, then turned his attention immediately to the host before Hermione could open her mouth to reply. ÊIs there a reason my companion wasnıt seated immediately upon her arrival?Ë He affected the iciest tone possible and raised a disbelieving brow at the older gentleman, who had the awareness to look momentarily abashed. ÊApologies, sir, I had no idea the young lady was dining with you.Ë Draco sneered, not believing the slimy wizard for a minute. ÊIndeed. Granger did you inform this man we had a reservation this evening under my name?Ë He turned back toward Hermione and noticed she was turning rather pink in the face. ÊI did, but Malfoy you donıt have toÐË ÊI thought as much,Ë he cut her off and turned back to the host. ÊNow, why donıt you make yourself useful and show us to our table.Ë The man nodded meekly and levitated menus in front of him as he led Draco and Hermione to a dimly lit back room. The whole restaurant was decorated in rich burgundy and dark gray furnishings with mahogany tables, chairs, and pillars. Paintings of all sizes adorned the walls, each containing an image of a blood red rose that revolved slowly and almost pulsed inward on itself, the red paint of the petals so lustrous Draco had often wondered if he touched the painting whether his hand would come away wet. The front dining room was full, but Draco had secured a more private table in the back room, hoping it might make Hermione more comfortable to be seen out in public with him. Now he realized this had all been a giant mistake. No one in this restaurant would pay them any mind anyway: it was mostly old families and no one from the Prophet was allowed on the premises. Thatıs what made upscale, exclusive pureblood businesses so successful: the promise of an extravagant experience under a modest cloak of privacy. If pureblood families wanted notoriety and attention, they knew how to get it, especially from the high society lapdogs in the wizarding press. But places like The Wilting Rose catered to the more refined and secretive inclinations of the old families. Why he thought this would be a good outing with Granger to test the friendship waters, Draco had no idea and was stewing with regret. When Hermione had removed the outer cloak of her robes, Draco practically tore it from her hands and shoved it and his roughly at the host. ÊPlease do take care with these. Also, I think a bottle of your finest elf-made wine for the trouble you gave my companion wouldnıt be amiss, now would it?Ë Draco snarled and the host blanched, muttered something that sounded like ÊRight away, sir,Ë and scuttled off. Draco took a deep breath and sat down across from Hermione. ÊYou didnıt have to do all that,Ë she said quietly. He scoffed. ÊI absolutely did, that little flobberworm needed to be put in his place.Ë Hermione let out a giggle and Draco frowned. ÊWhatıs the joke?Ë She gave him an apologetic smile, but quickly divulged, ÊIt reminded me a bit of when we were in school together. I swear the next words out of your mouth were going to be Èwait until my father hears about this!ıË Draco chuckled at her teasing and felt some of the tension leave his body. ÊWell, luckily for me, that wouldnıt be possible.Ë Hermioneıs face paled and she looked completely ashamed. ÊOh Merlin, Malfoy Iım so sorry, I forgot. I didnıt mean╔ anything by it╔Ë she trailed off awkwardly and Draco averted his eyes in shame. She was sorry? Sorry that his bastard father was dead? The man had personally tried to murder and/or maim her own friends on more than one occasion and she was sorry? No, this would not do. Draco couldnıt hold it in anymore. Before he could open his mouth, a wine bottle gracefully floated down on the table and two crystal wine glasses appeared in front of them. The cork jumped from the bottle and the airborne carafe proceeded to pour a generous measure into each of their glasses. Draco downed half his glass of the ridiculous vintage (1876, damn) and noted that Hermione had done the same. Apparently they both needed the liquid courage provided from practically chugging a rather expensive glass of wine in one go. She was looking anywhere but at him and Draco knew this was the time. It was now or never to finally seek his penance from the one person who could truly grant him absolution. He hadnıt planned on the night coming to this, but then sheıd gone and been that selfless caring person she always was, and Draco was going to burst with the guilt. Countless healing appointments had brought Draco to the point where he finally felt ready to exorcise the demons surrounding his treatment of Hermione Granger. And with no Calming Draughts, no potions at all in his system, he charged ahead. Draco set his wine glass down firmly. Her brown eyes finally met his and he sought courage in their warmth. I am in control of this. Draco took one last deep breath, and then he took the plunge. ÊI need to get this all out and Granger, I know how you like interrupting and asking questions when an idea strikes, but please, please, if I donıt get this all out now, Iım not sure Iıll ever be able to.Ë She nodded, her eyes wide and strangely bright. He took a deep breath and a generous glug of wine. The bottle got the hint that he was low on liquid and floated over to refill his glass and then Hermioneıs in turn. You can do this. You have to fucking do this one thing. ÊI owe you, probably, a lifetimeıs worth of apologies. When I was a bratty school boy and I went to Hogwarts thinking I was better than everyone╔ I treated you horribly. Those inherited values and prejudice, courtesy of my family╔ it shaped everything about my childhood. I honestly believed I was better than you because of my blood status. But a tiny part of me, even then, knew it was all bollocks. When you beat me on every exam, when you were top in every class╔ I started running out of excuses for why you were inferior. So I stuck with the only thing I could hate about you: your lineage. It was pettiness, plain and simple, Granger. You were better at magic and it burned me up inside. And instead of respecting you for it, I was going to tear you down the only way I knew how: with slurs about you and your family. Reminding you, and everyone else like you, that you could never be an equal in our world built me up for a while, and I could go on pretending that any of that mattered. But it was all bollocks╔ I know that now.Ë He leaned closer over the table, earnest in expunging these long buried confessions. She was hanging on his every word. The intensity of her eyes both encouraged and frightened him simultaneously. ÊAs a kid, you were this impossible thing to me. How could someone my father told me was dirty, stupid, less than me╔ how could that person be so brilliant? Everything about magic and our classes, all the friends you had╔ every single thing seemed to come so easily to you. I couldnıt square the two realities in my head so I picked the lazy option. I chose to belittle you and threw myself into hating you and everything you and Potter represented. It wasnıt right, and it scares me when I think about how easy it was for me to live in that hatred.Ë He finally paused for a breath. He gulped the wine again and noticed his hand was trembling. The next part might be his undoing and his body was warning him of the impending stress. Hermioneıs hand twitched on the table between them and he wondered if sheıd thought about grabbing his hand in hers. Draco set his glass down and clutched his hands in his lap. He didnıt deserve her kindness. ÊIım sorry for how I treated you in school and for the horrible, awful things I said. Iım sorry I ever called you Mudblood. Iım sorry I ever fucking thought that word in association with you. But most of all, I wish I could take back that night at the Manor.Ë This was killing him inside, ripping open fresh wounds that had scarcely begun to heal, but he forced himself to hold eye contact. The longer Draco talked, the more it felt like when Potter hit him with Sectumsempra all over again, small and large gashes across his body, bleeding him dry. ÊI could hear you. I couldnıt bring myself to watch, but I could hear her torturing you and I did nothing. Iıll never be able to take back the fact that I stood by while you screamed, while you suffered. I still have nightmares about what happened that night╔ because I should have done something, done anything, but I was a coward. You should hate me.Ë The next words he spoke pained him, but she needed to know he wouldnıt hold her immediate actions against her. ÊAnd if after all that, you wanted to walk out of here tonight and never see me again╔ I would understand.Ë Her eyes still retained that odd, bright quality as silence fell between them. ÊIs that what you want?Ë Her voice was barely above a whisper. ÊSorry?Ë ÊIs that what you want?Ë Hermione repeated in that same quiet voice. ÊHas that what all this has been about? Has all our time together in the mornings just been about you working up to this moment?Ë ÊNo.Ë His response was firm, but his voice hoarse with emotion. No, of course not, how could you think that, Granger? Mornings with you are the only reason I leave my bed. I had to do the honorable thing and offer you a way out, please, please, donıt fucking leave. ÊI just╔ couldnıt stand seeing you every day, pretending we didnıt have this history. And before I blurted it all out at the wrong time and fucked it all up, if I didnıt tell you how I regretted everything╔ it was eating at me constantly, Granger. Itıs no good acting as if we were simply old school acquaintances.Ë His breath felt like it was coming in short bursts. He finished his wine and looked down at the table, feeling spent. She had the option to leave, but that didnıt mean he had to watch her go. Always the selfish coward. ÊI forgive you,Ë she said quietly. ÊAnd I donıt want to walk out. I donıt want to stop seeing you in the morning.Ë That swelling feeling of hope filled his chest cavity as her soft words seemed to penetrate his very soul. Her decree of forgiveness coursed through his veins, thrumming along with his magic and building up a powerful wave of emotion. He didnıt know how to express the depth of his gratitude, so Draco merely nodded as he found her gaze again and noticed a tear escape her full eyes. She didnıt bother wiping it away. Hermione cleared her throat. ÊYouıre wrong, though, about a few things.Ë She took a shaky breath. ÊThings were never easy for me, far from it. There were times when I was with Harry during that last year of the war that I felt like giving up. We had this impossible task in front of us and there were times I wanted to fall asleep at night and not wake up the next day. I couldnıt tell Harry or Ron, of course, but some days it all felt so monumentally unfair. The things we were expected to do.Ë She paused here and gave him a pained smile. Another tear leaked out. ÊWe were children. All of us. You, me, my friends╔Ë she whispered and Draco detected a bitterness to her voice. She cleared her throat and adopted her usual brisk and bossy affect. ÊAnd as for your Èinherited values and prejudiceı well that simply is not true. Prejudice isnıt inherited, itıs taught. Children, people, are taught to hate. No one enters this world with those types of preconceived beliefs. Make no mistake, Malfoy, it is akin to abuse, forcing those blood purity ideals onto children.Ë Draco shook his head. Though he couldnıt describe the relief coursing through him that she wasnıt completely disgusted with his existence, she needed to know he wasnıt a good person. Why arenıt you scared of me Granger? Iım the bad guy in our story. ÊI had a choice though, didnıt I? Eventually I was old enough to know better,Ë he countered bitterly. Hermione nodded thoughtfully. ÊTrue. But you know better now. Itıs never too late to make the right choice.Ë She was being far too lenient and it unnerved him. Why donıt you run from me? Havenıt I shown you already? Iım the villain. You screamed and writhed and suffered mere feet from me and I did nothing. ÊI could have helped youÐË She cut him off abruptly. ÊVoldemort would have killed you. You and your entire family.Ë ÊMaybe he should have.Ë ÊDonıt say that.Ë She responded immediately in a severe tone, giving Draco a sliver of hope that she might actually care about him in some small way. Her eyes still shone but it seemed her tears were done falling. Draco leaned back in his seat and ran a hand through his hair. ÊWell, this wasnıt how I imagined my Friday night,Ë he said and earned a shaky laugh from Hermione. She dabbed her eyes completely dry and when she looked up again her expression cleared. ÊHow about we get another bottle?Ë Draco happily obliged and motioned to a passing waiter. When they had two full glasses in front of them again, Hermione raised her glass toward him. ÊTo old-new friends.Ë He clinked his glass against hers, but then set it down. ÊAre we? Friends?Ë Hermione cocked her head to one side; a question in her eyes. ÊI thought so.Ë Draco nodded. ÊAll right then. Friends.Ë The word felt odd coming out of his mouth, though not unpleasantly so. A calm settled over their dark table at last, and they both visibly relaxed. After the emotional start to the evening, things took a turn for the normal as they ordered entrees and talked about their respective work days. At Hermioneıs suggestion, they took a walk through the theatre district after dinner. Despite the fact that it was late in the November evening, neither Draco nor Hermione felt particularly cold, especially after splitting two bottles of wine over the course of the meal. For his part, Draco was glad to oblige her suggestion of a walk, if only to clear the slight buzz in his system. They walked in comfortable silence for a few minutes, close enough that their shoulders were brushing, but neither of them minded. If he concentrated, not that he should, Draco could catch that mysterious floral scent of her hair. Hermione broke the silence first. ÊAbout what you said at dinner╔Ë she trailed off and Draco raised his eyebrows and he looked down at her, waiting for her to continue. ÊThank you for apologizing. I know that was probably very difficult for you.Ë Draco shrugged and averted his eyes. You have no bloody clue, Granger. But I had to try. ÊIt was necessary,Ë he responded stiffly. Hermione nodded. ÊStill, it was appreciated.Ë They began walking along the cobblestones again at a slower pace. The November air was frosty, but Draco still felt pleasantly warm even without casting a charm. Whether it was from the wine, her words, or her company, he couldnıt say, and would rather not dwell on it at all. She spoke softly again. ÊDespite what you may have thought, I never hated you, you know. It was more a feeling of╔ disappointment.Ë ÊHow do you mean?Ë Dracoıs heartbeat quickened, wondering if the axe was about to fall. They came to a stop in front of the ballet theater. Hermione turned to look up fully into his face with a pensive expression. ÊWhen you bullied me at school, I could never bring myself to feel actual hatred for you. Obviously, I didnıt particularly like you, as you did quite a lot of damage to my confidence and I shed more than enough tears over you in my early school years╔Ë Draco cringed at the memories. Merlin, heıd been a prick, hadnıt he? ÊBut it always seemed to me that you had a lot more to offer the world than the life your father wished for you. I know you earned good marks in school, and getting to know you has allowed me to see that youıre intelligent, discerning, and ambitious. I want you to know that I saw all that potential in you at a young age, but every time you opened your mouth and spouted off the bloody purity nonsense or goaded Ron for his familyıs lack of money, it left me feeling disappointed that you would choose to waste your self-worth in that way.Ë His breath hitched. Her assessment of him was one of the most honest things Draco had ever heard from another person. How could Granger be so comfortable sharing this with him? This must be what it was like to have an actual friend. She hadnıt let him entirely off the hook, nor should she, but sheıd also listed off things she admired about him. She believed in him. Draco couldnıt remember the last time someone had paid him a genuine compliment. Draco swallowed thickly. ÊThank you, Granger. That means a lot╔ most especially coming from you. Iım so sorry I everÐË She held up a hand to pause his contrite speech. ÊMalfoy, look, Iıve had enough of apologies for tonight. Truthfully, I forgave you a long time ago for your childish behavior. Trust me,Ë she let out a hollow laugh. ÊI saw enough real horror in my life to dwell too long on your old prejudices from when we were children.Ë Draco watched as she hugged her arms around herself and turned toward the brightly lit front of the ballet theater. Her brown eyes were staring at something faraway, it seemed. ÊI also still have nightmares about what your aunt did to me in your home,Ë she continued and Draco bit the inside of his cheek and felt his fists clench, a mixture of both shame and fear radiating through him. ÊAnd I do forgive you, I do. I know the impossible position you were in with your parents. Iıd still like to talk about it, someday, when weıre both ready.Ë She turned back to face him, and Draco was puzzled to see a small smile playing about her lips. ÊJust not tonight. Iım having too nice a time.Ë ÊYou are?Ë he blurted the question and cursed himself for his lack of control. Why did Granger always have him feeling so off kilter? I am in control of this. ÊI am. Iım glad weıre friends, Malfoy.Ë ÊMe too, Granger.Ë She gave him another genuine smile and Draco experienced that body-wide warm feeling again. He noticed Granger casting her eyes about and looking slightly awkward. She must be trying to find a way to gently end the evening. How did one usually part from a friend? Draco had a feeling the Êlater, mateË he exchanged with Theo wouldnıt quite do for Granger. As he struggled internally with how to perform a basic social interaction, he almost missed her speaking up. ÊThe ballet is back in season after the holidays.Ë Hermione gestured toward the large, moving posters of dancers adorning the darkened theater doors. ÊThe Moscow ballet is one of the best and it looks like theyıre sticking with a classic show,Ë he offered. ÊI wouldnıt know, Iıve never been to the wizarding ballet,Ë she admitted quietly. ÊSeriously?Ë he arched a brow at her and she nodded. ÊI used to go to Muggle ballets with my parents when I was younger. We made sure to see a performance of The Nutcracker around Christmas every year,Ë she said, smiling wistfully. Draco wrinkled his nose but decided against saying anything negative about Muggle ballets with ridiculous sounding names. The Nutcracker? ÊWe should go then. My family are still patrons of this theater, so Iım fairly certain we have season tickets.Ë ÊWait, really? You have season tickets to the ballet and youıd take me?Ë Draco shrugged and tried to appear nonchalant. He hadnıt gone to a ballet since he was a small child and truth be told, it bored him to tears. But Granger looked so bloody excited, how could he not offer this to her? That was what friendship was all about, right? But maybe she didnıt want to go with him. Be seen with him in public. He scuffed the toe of his dragon-hide shoe against the cobblestone, and stuffed his hands in his pockets. ÊOf course, you could take one of your female friends instead. Maybe Weasley or Lovegood?Ë Hermione snorted a laugh. ÊItıs Potter, and good lord no, I canıt take either of them. Ginny would rather be force-fed gillyweed and Luna is convinced that ballets are a conspiracy invented by gazelles to make humans feel self-conscious.Ë ÊSorry, what?Ë ÊNever mind. The point is, youıre the only friend that would willingly accompany me. Please? Iıll even pay you for my ticket.Ë Draco waved an impatient hand and tried to ignore the swooping sensation in his stomach at the way sheıd said ÊPlease.Ë ÊDonıt be ridiculous. Fine, Iıll take you to the damn ballet. Pick a date in January or February and weıll go.Ë Hermione clapped her hands together excitedly and Draco tried to ignore the light feeling in his chest at the thought heıd made her happy. He sure was doing a lot of ignoring and suppressing of feelings tonight. I am in control of this. ÊExcellent! Iıll select the night and let you know Monday.Ë She looked up into his face, her smile wide and sincere. ÊThis was nice, Malfoy, thanks for suggesting dinner.Ë He inclined his head toward her. ÊNot at all Granger,Ë he murmured. Hermione took a step back and gave him a little wave. ÊI suppose Iıll see you Monday then. Enjoy the rest of your weekend.Ë He merely gave her a friendly nod before she apparated away and left Draco staring at the empty space she had occupied just moments before. When he returned to his own home moments later, two letters were waiting for him. The first and more exciting letter was from McGonagall. The school governors had accepted her motion to review the fund for children of non-magical parents, and he should receive a list of comments from them after the New Year. Setting the good news aside, he opened the second, less thrilling piece of post. It was a letter from his mother, currently in France, asking if he might like to visit before she returned to England for the holiday season. Draco took his motherıs letter to the writing desk of his bedroom. He penned Narcissa a cordial rejection, saying he would be too busy with social obligations for work (this was sort of true) in the upcoming weeks as the Christmas season ramped up and that he could unfortunately not spare the time for a visit. He wrote that he looked forward to her return and pointedly did not address Narcissaıs paragraph detailing the names and attributes of several young, French witches who had asked after his well-being. Draco snorted aloud as he imagined his motherıs reaction to his dinner out with Granger. Yes, Mother, Iım sure those lovely young ladies are concerned about my actual well-being and not whether if a marriage to me would result in a key to the Malfoy vaults at Gringotts. By the way, I just spent the evening in the company of my friend, Hermione Granger. You might know her as being a war heroine and the young woman who was tortured by your sister on our manorıs drawing room floor? Yes, well our time together generally consists of intellectual stimulation at a level most humans dream of because sheıs bloody brilliant andÐ Obviously, he would not be writing that to his mother tonight. Instead he included a brief paragraph about his job, and whistled for his owl. Chapter End Notes A/N: Fun fact about this particular chapter, the apology scene at the restaurant was the scene that jumpstarted this entire fic. Years (oh lord, years and years) ago, I imagined what it would be like for a character like Draco to sincerely apologize to Hermione╔ and then I thought about how they would even arrive at that moment and how would this affect them after and then it all spiraled into this gigantic slow-burn story that Iım finally able to share. I canıt begin to express how happy all your interactions with this story make feel, so once again, thank you thank you thank you. chapter art by flyora. Chapter 9: Chapter 9 Chapter Notes See the end of the chapter for notes The second there was a break after Molly Weasleyıs impeccable roast dinner and before dessert could be brought to the table, Hermione cast a very significant look down the table at Ginny. She and Ginny didnıt even bother coming up with a pretense for why they both needed to leave the table and go to the pantry off the Burrowıs kitchen at the same time. No one in their generation had gotten a proper childhood or adolescence. School children turned into soldiers and weapons. Hermione sighed and hugged her arms around her body and stared at the floor of the pantry. ÊForgiving him was the easy part, but I was worried that once I did, that it would all be over. Harry and Ron stood flanking the small doorway, arms crossed with identical devilish grins on their faces. Harry quickly dismissed Hermioneıs sound-interfering charm. ÊWell, well, whatıs this we hear my wife shouting about? ÊSo was it just dinner?Ë Ginny shot him a quelling glare. ÊHarry, I wonıt be put in this position again. This is just like the Yule Ball fiasco with Krum. ÊPlease, both of you, trust her judgment.Ë The boys exchanged small frowns but shrugged in agreement. ÊPromise youıll hex this guyıs balls off if he hurts her?Ë asked Ron. ÊOh, Iıd take quite a bit of pleasure in doing that again,Ë chuckled Ginny. Ron left then but Harry shot his wife a questioning glance before he followed suit. It took a moment for Ginny to realize her verbal slip-up. Damn, Harry was often too perceptive for his own good. Praying her husband wouldnıt think too hard about her comment and start making assumptions, Ginny joined the rest of the family back in the dining room. Maybe it was time to dig up that mobile phone Hermione had gifted her last Christmas if they wanted to talk more privately about Malfoy╔ December 2007 Now that Draco counted Granger as an official friend, he found it put less pressure on his daily interactions with her. He no longer winced when she said the words ÊsorryË or Êthank youË and he could offer them as well in conversation without breaking into a sweat. Like a normal, adult person. For example: ÊSorry, Granger, could I borrow your pen thing for a minute? Thanks.Ë Or, Êsorry, but Malfoy could you look at this last line of these new crup-breeding regulations? Thank you, I donıt think the wording is quite right.Ë Maybe Healer Browning had been right about apologizing after all. Draco hadnıt needed a Calming Draught in weeks, but did anticipate he would need one for the coming weekend. The second weekend of every December, Dracoıs employers at Whisp & Wright threw an extravagant Christmas gala, inviting all the quidditch elite. Employees were expected to attend, and youıd have to be dead to not want to be seen at this particular soiree. Though Draco was still counted among the living, he dreaded this party every year. He never took a date, spent the required amount of polite time schmoozing with the Waspsı staff and players, then downed as much free Ogdenıs as he could before ending up pissed and Flooing back home or to Theoıs place. Come to think of it, he really should owl his friend. He hadnıt seen him in months, and spending proper drunken time together was definitely in order. But Theo had replied to Dracoıs most recent owl saying he unfortunately already had plans that particular evening. Draco frowned as he read the letter. It was unlike his friend to be so vague. Draco was ruminating about Theoıs mysterious absence from his life as of late, when Grangerıs impatient huff broke through. ÊGinny told me she canıt come to the Ministry gala this weekend. That means I have to play Harryıs bodyguard all night.Ë Draco smirked. ÊPoor Potter canıt defend himself from the onslaught of female admirers on his own, eh?Ë Hermione rolled her eyes. ÊYou have no idea. Harry is so bloody polite to everyone, they think heıs truly interested. People practically grope him when Ginnyıs not around! One year, some drunken witch actually snuck up behind him and snipped off a lock of his hair! A security wizard stunned her before she could run off.Ë Draco burst out laughing and Hermione followed suit. ÊI can actually picture that gitıs horrified face.Ë As their laughter died out and they sipped their morning beverages, Dracoıs mind began to picture something else. What did Granger look like when she attended the Ministry Christmas gala? Muggle style gowns were all the rage these days, with witches learning how to tastefully wear dresses that exposed much more skin than the usual dress robe. Which way did Granger lean, fashion-wise? Strapless ball gown or opulent robes? And what would she do with her hair? Draco, and probably every other boy at Hogwarts, remembered well her sleek up-do from their Fourth Year at the Yule Ball. Or perhaps she would leave it down for the evening, her soft-looking waves spilling down the inviting bare skin of her shoulders and back╔ Stop. No. Granger is your friend. These were not thoughts one had about a friend. Especially such a recent friend. Especially such a recent friend who would most likely not take kindly to any sort of sordid thoughts or feelings about her attractiveness from her former childhood bully. Merlin, help him, did he have to be such a creep? But thank Salazar for small mercies. Dracoıs employers purposely scheduled their annual gala for the same night as the Ministry one. Having found over the years that combining government officials, quidditch stars, and copious amounts of free alcohol led to slimy behavior and backroom dealing from both parties, the galas conflicted with one another as an avoidance tactic. Which meant Draco didnıt have to fret about whether or not it would be appropriate to ask Granger if sheıd like to attend his companyıs party with him. Of course that didnıt stop his mind from conjuring the image of her clutching his arm as he introduced her to people, or how she might lead him over to the dance floor where he could pull her close and╔ Stop. No. I am in control of this. Draco leaned up against the marble bar, sipping on his second drink of the evening. Heıd exhausted all his social charm between various Wasp players, their spouses, reserve players, coaching staff, team owners, and trainers. Now he could quietly stand by the bar alone, enjoy some drinks courtesy of his employer, and silently judge everyone in the room until it became a socially acceptable hour to leave. One of Dracoıs favorite train wrecks, Cormac McLaggen, was well on his way to making a spectacular fool of himself. This would be the moronıs final week with the agency, as word somehow (Draco would bet half his vaults on Macnair being involved) reached the partners about all the lecherous activity he got up to with the prospects he was supposed to be scouting for the Harpies and suddenly, McLaggen found himself removed from his post. He spent all week talking up the new job his Uncle Tiberius had secured for him at the Ministry, as if the entire office didnıt already know the real reason for his sudden departure. Bellamy had swiftly promoted Fiona Moy in McLaggenıs place and given that the woman had actually played Keeper for the Harpies before retiring to scout, Draco knew heıd probably have stiff competition at roster-building come next season. And even though Cormac was no longer technically employed at Whisp & Wright, it hadnıt stopped him from showing up to the holiday gala. The poor excuse for a wizard had danced rather lewdly with both Beaters from the Harpies, almost spilled his drink on the Keeper for the Ballycastle Bats, and had just now made an ill-advised attempt to place his hands on the shoulders of a red-headed woman in a stunning silvery dress. Ginny Potterıs wand was at McLaggenıs throat faster than he could blink. Draco chuckled into his drink as the gaping moron retracted his wandering hands and stammered an apology. The She-Weasel whispered something threatening, based on the oafıs terrified expression, then turned abruptly on her heel to stalk toward the bar. Her eyes caught Dracoıs and her face grew into a smirk as she stowed her wand away. Draco thought she might just give him a polite nod as she usually did when they crossed paths in public. The hug he would have to think about later, because Draco did not need to be any more indebted to Hermione Granger and so he needed to focus on how to repay this Christmas kindness somehow. This was why Draco found himself tearing through wizarding London on one of the busiest shopping days of the year. Where the fuck should he even begin? Jewelry was a laughable option. That was far too serious for a friend, and outside of a few small, delicate looking pieces, she didnıt seem to wear much of it anyway. Dress robes? Dear Merlin, no, heıd probably suffer a public panic attack if he had to discuss his best guess at Grangerıs measurements with a shop proprietor. Sweets wouldnıt be enough, and he couldnıt be sure what her favorite candies were either. He made a mental note to ask her soon. Wine? Flowers? Both options were too impersonal. Come on, fucking think. What was Grangerıs passion? As if the universe wanted to supply the answer for him, Draco found his feet had landed him right outside Flourish and Blotts. Of course, books! Almost an hour later of squeezing past other patrons of the busy book shop and combing through every genre of every section, Draco was no closer to selecting a gift for Hermione. Every time he picked up a book and read the title, he would eventually place it back with a sigh. Sheıd most likely read every book in here, how the hell was he supposed to select one she didnıt already own? He needed something more original than a stupid book. Circling round to the back again, his eyes landed on a table of handsome leather journals. Picking up two of them, he turned them over in his hands, an idea taking shape. It would take a significant amount of charms work on his part, but he was confident enough in his skills that he could get the journals to do what he needed. Smirking to himself, he completed his purchase and left the bookstore. Another idea struck him as he stepped back out onto the crowded streets and he knew there was one more stop to make before going home. ÊHello Draco Malfoy!Ë called a cheerful, dreamy voice. Draco stopped dead in his tracks as a young woman with long blonde hair and protuberant blue eyes approached him with a confident smile. Loony? No, Granger said that was cruel╔ Luna? Luna Lovegood. Then Draco remembered with a swooping sense of shame that the smiling witch before him was once imprisoned in his Manorıs dungeon for a few months. ÊErm, hello Lovegood,Ë he tentatively replied. Her smile, if it were possible, grew even wider. ÊOh good, you do recognize me! You can call me Luna, if you please. Itıs been a number of years though, hasnıt it? Since weıve seen one another?Ë ÊI suppose it has,Ë he offered, completely unsure of himself or why this witch had bothered to flag him down in the middle of the street. Luna cocked her head to the side and narrowed her wide eyes slightly. ÊDid you just buy a present for Hermione?Ë What the fuck? ÊIÐI mean, I╔ perhaps I did pick up something for Granger, whatıs it to you?Ë If Luna Lovegood was bothered at all by his aggressive tone, she didnıt show it. She simply shrugged carelessly. ÊI saw you coming out of her favorite store just now. You also seem to be in rather a hurry, much like the rest of the last-minute Christmas shoppers today, so I assumed you were picking up a gift for someone. Plus, I am aware that the two of you are friends now, which is quite nice, in my opinion.Ë ÊGranger told you weıre friends?Ë Luna only offered another shrug and those slightly mad, wide eyes. ÊNot personally, no. But I often overhear her talking with Ginny about you when weıre all at the Burrow. Sometimes even my friends can be forgetful of the fact that I have two working ears. They used to be better about hiding their conversations. You seem to be a most perplexing creature, but then Hermione also has been quite happy as of late too, which I gather is your doing.Ë Draco opened and closed his mouth, lost for words. ÊYou know, people always say we look related, but I think my shade is much more yellow.Ë Without any further warning she walked right into Dracoıs personal space and lifted a bunch of her hair against the side of his head. ÊHmm, see I was right. My blonde is much more yellow than yours.Ë She took a step back and gave him an appraising stare. ÊLovegood, what the hell was that?Ë ÊWould you like to join me at the Hogıs Head?Ë ÊWould I like to╔ what?Ë Luna Lovegood actually had the audacity after her weird little hair stunt to cross her arms impatiently over her chest and appear annoyed at having to repeat her abrupt and insane question. ÊWould you like to join me at the Hogıs Head? Iım meeting Ginny and quite a few other friends for drinks and general merriment. Would you like to come and celebrate in the Yuletide spirit?Ë Draco didnıt know whether to laugh at her, shout at her, or simply turn on his heel and leave her in the middle of the street, babbling nonsense. ÊNo thanks LovegoodÐË ÊLuna.Ë ÊRight, no thanks, Luna, Iıve still got to finish my shopping.Ë He could not get away from this odd witch fast enough. How the hell was Granger friends with this person? The same could be said of you, could it not? ÊOh well, perhaps another time when you will feel more welcome. I hope Hermione enjoys the gift you bought. Have a safe and festive Christmas!Ë She left Draco muttering under his breath and began walking away. He counted to ten breaths before he cursed the very day he was ever born before calling her back. ÊLovegood! Wait, please.Ë ÊLuna.Ë ÊWhatever. Listen, I just wanted to say that Iım sorry. Iım sorry for the time you were╔ forced to spend in my familyıs home.Ë He couldnıt even bring himself to say the word Êimprisoned.Ë Coward. ÊOh yes I remember!Ë She exclaimed happily, as if heıd just brought up some pleasant memory of them bonding over pygmy puffs. ÊRight, well, sorry about╔ all that. Enjoy your evening with Weasley.Ë Draco made to turn away, but her small hand quickly grabbed his own. ÊItıs Potter. And I do rather think you were as much a prisoner of the Manor as me. Farewell Draco Malfoy!Ë Dropping his hand, she gave him a cheery wave and skipped down the street, completely oblivious to the perplexed stares of passerby. The door to the Hogıs Head creaked open and Ginny looked up excitedly to see Luna stroll in from the snowy street. ÊHey Luna! Got you a butterbeer already,Ë Ginny slid the drink toward her friend. Luna considered the drink but then shook her head. ÊHmm, I think Iıll trouble Aberforth for a gillywater instead. Rolf is home from his trip tomorrow and I think weıre going to start trying for a child.Ë Ginny blinked back at her friend in surprise. ÊAll right then, I guess I can finish this one off as well. Did you just apparate from Diagon?Ë ÊYes, I was performing my annual check for nargles at the apothecary. Levels were low this year, which is a good sign. Oh and I had a lovely conversation with Draco Malfoy.Ë Ginnyıs jaw dropped as Luna glided up to the bar to receive her non-alcoholic beverage. When she slid back next to Ginny, the redhead didnıt know which question she wanted to ask first of the million that were on her tongue. ÊYou ran into Malfoy? How uhh╔ well how did that go?Ë ÊQuite well!Ë exclaimed Luna. ÊI think I left him a little flustered after he apologized to me. Oh, and I invited him to join us here.Ë Ginny whipped her head toward the door, expecting to see a smirking pale face walk through, but none came. ÊDonıt worry Ginny, he politely declined. He was busy finishing up his Christmas shopping for Hermione.Ë Throughout her years-long friendship with Luna, Ginny liked to think she had become quite immune to her friend dropping ridiculous statements. Most of her creature or conspiracy related posturing hardly raised an eyebrow from her anymore. But Luna dropping into conversation that Draco Malfoy was running around a few days before Christmas trying to buy Hermione a gift? Well that earned another jaw drop. Before Ginny could compose herself enough to interrogate Luna, a boisterous voice called out to them. ÊOi! You two started drinking without us?Ë Seamus strode up to the table with a teasing grin, accompanied by Dean, Parvati, and Padma. Ginny and Luna made room around the table, as their old Dumbledoreıs Army comrades joined the party. Strong bonds had formed amongst the group during the awful last year of the war. Under Nevilleıs leadership, this ragtag group of students left behind at Hogwarts grew close as they sought in their own way to support Harry, Ron, and Hermioneıs mission. Roughly once a month, as many of the group as possible tried to get together at the Hogıs Head to catch up and irritate Aberforth. Soon their group had taken over most of the bar, with the arrival of Susan Bones, Ernie MacMillan, Hannah Longbottom (nee Abbott), Terry Boot, Cho Chang, Michael Corner, Justin Finch-Fletchley, Demelza Robbins, and Jimmy Peakes. Several rounds of butterbeer and firewhisky later, and Parvati produced her magazineıs latest issue, containing the always hotly-debated list of Most Eligible Wizarding Bachelors to the delight of the women in the group. ÊSeriously Parvati, thank you for finally getting Ron off the list. Weıve only been dating for four bloody years.Ë Padma complained wryly. ÊYouıre just mad because of how smug he was for months afterward,Ë snickered Ginny, earning an eye roll from Padma as she muttered Êinsufferable git, Iıll show him ÈeligibleıË under her breath. ÊYes well, I reminded Romilda Vane that she owed me a favor after I convinced Madame Coursant to design her robes for the Ministry gala,Ë replied Parvati coyly, flipping through the spread. Parvati was well connected in the wizarding fashion world as the fashion correspondent for Witch Weekly. ÊLooks like your brother Charlie is the only Weasley this year, Gin,Ë remarked Susan, and turning the page, she wrinkled her nose. ÊUgh, Romilda included Cormac McLaggen? That man is a seriously uncouth prick.Ë ÊIf you think thatıs bad, flip back to page 36,Ë clipped Parvati. The female section of the party all bent their heads to huddle over the magazine as Susan dutifully returned to page 36. Ginnyıs stomach dropped as she was confronted once again, with the subject of too many of her conversations with her friends these days: Draco sodding Malfoy. The magazine had only included a few photos that Ginny could tell came from his appearances at recent quidditch matches for work. Other men on the list, like McLaggen, had actually volunteered to be photographed personally for their pages. ÊHuh.Ë Remarked Cho, after a few beats of silence. No one else seemed brave enough to offer an opinion one way or another. ÊAll right, Iıll say it for the group, the man is ridiculously good-looking,Ë said Susan firmly. Demelza let out a frustrated sigh. ÊYouıre right. Heıs fit. Very, very fit.Ë Parvati tutted in disapproval. ÊCome on, none of you are taking this seriously. Malfoy was a Death Eater, lest we all forget. I canıt believe Romilda thought this was appropriate! He let them into our school!Ë Ginny bit her own tongue, tamping down the urge to speak up in Malfoyıs defense. She knew that Lavenderıs death at the hands of Fenrir Greyback still haunted Parvati, and anyone associated with that depraved werewolf or his Death Eater cronies was a sore point for her. Ginny didnıt much feel like arguing on Malfoyıs behalf, no matter how tragic his circumstances had been, when she still was working through her own feelings on his apparent redeemed character. Surprisingly, Susan took up the mantle instead. ÊHe was a scared, stupid kid Parvati,Ë she began gently. ÊAnd I donıt know how many of you have spent time with him recently, but I worked on a project with him this summer and fall and he was nothing but professional.Ë The group turned to Susan in surprise and she shrugged. ÊHe helped my department organize the international charity quidditch match with France. Not only is he fluent in French, up close you can observe just how form-fitting those dark, tailored suits are under the robes.Ë Most of the women released tense giggles and the subject of Malfoy was finally dropped. But Ginnyıs mind couldnıt help but wander back to try and piece together all this new information from Hermione, Luna, Maureen Tyler, and now Susan Bones. Really racking up those goodwill points these days, arenıt we Malfoy? Merlin, maybe nifflers have indeed learned to fly╔ Chapter End Notes A/N: Thank you all for reading. The world outside is a stressful place, but this fandom brings me a lot of joy.
